content.full_text:
May 7, 1979

Mr. Frederick K. Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison and Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, California 94120

Dear Mr. Lowell:

Thank you for your letter of April 20, 1979. In that letter you
asked whether specific activities of staff attorneys rendering
services to clients would be included in determining if the attorneys
qualified as lobbyists. Your particular concern focused upon the
time test set out in 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(e)A)1. That test
provides that a person meets the "substantial or regular" definition
of a lobbyist if the person spends a total of 40 hours, including 10
hours of direct communication influencing or attempting to influence
legislative or administrative action.

You were correct in your analysis that certain activities are not
includable in determining time spent "influencing or attempting to
influence legislative or administrative action" and "communicating
directly" with respect to the 40/10 test. Those exclusions are 1)
Administrative testimony (unless the person providing the testimony
has, during the preceding six calendar months, made or arranged for
a gift of $10 or more in value to any legislative official or agency
official to whom the testimony is submitted), 2) travel time, and
3) time spent on research, i.e., the gathering of information, statis-
tics or analysis, the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs, memor-
anda or the preparation of bill analysis (2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239
(e)(3)(A)).

The specific activities you presented included:

   (1)  Engaging in legal research.

   (2)  Analyzing various studies pertaining to the regulation.

   (3)  Educating and preparing the client for off the record
        negotiations with the agency.

   (4)  Suggesting language to be included in the administrative
        testimony.

   (5)  Outlining various strategies for presentation of adminis-
        trative testimony.

   (6)  Meeting with the client to discuss the above matters.

Mr. Frederick K. Lowell
May 7, 1979
Page Two

Clearly, items (1) and (2) would be considered time spent on research
and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. Items (4) and
(5) would also be considered administrative testimony and therefore
not includable in the 40/10 time test. The inclusion of (3) as admin-
istrative testimony depends upon the purpose of the negotiations. I
cannot respond without more information regarding the type of negotia-
tions.

While some of the above items are clearly excludable from the 40/10
time test of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(e)(A)1., any time spent by an
attorney on items (4) and (5) above would be includable in the time
tests of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(2)(A)2. and 3.

Please contact me if I can be of further assistance.

Sincerely,


J. Richard Eichman
Accounting Specialist
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

May 7, 1979


Mr. Frederick K. Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison and Sutro
P.O. Box 7830
San Francisco, California 94120

Dear Mr. Lowell:

Thank you for your letter of April 20, 1979. In that letter you
asked whether specific activities of staff attorneys rendering
services to clients would be included in determining if the attorneys
qualified as lobbyists. Your particular concern focused upon the
time test set out in 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(e)(A)1. That test
provides that a person meets the "substantial or regular" definition
of a lobbyist if the person spends a total of 40 hours, including 10
hours of direct communication influencing or attempting to influence
legislative or administrative action.

You were correct in your analysis that certain activities are not
includable in determining time spent "influencing or attempting to
influence legislative or administrative action" and "communicating
directly" with respect to the 40/10 test. Those exclusions are 1)
Administrative testimony (unless the person providing the testimony
has, during the preceding six calendar months, made or arranged for
a gift of $10 or more in value to any legislative official or agency
official to whom the testimony is submitted) 2) travel time, and
3) time spent on research, i.e., the gathering of information, statis-
tics or analysis, the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs, memo-
randa or the preparation of bill analysis (2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239
(e)(3)(A)).

The specific activities you presented included:

     (1)  Engaging in legal research.

     (2)  Analyzing various studies pertaining to the regulation.

     (3)  Educating and preparing the client for off the record
          negotiations with the agency.

     (4)  Suggesting language to be included in the administrative
          testimony.

     (5)  Outlining various strategies for presentation of adminis-
          trative testimony.

     (6)  Meeting with the client to discuss the above matters.

Mr. Frederick K. Lowell
May 7, 1979
Page Two


Clearly, items (1) and (2) would be considered time spent on research
and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. Items (4) and
(5) would also be considered administrative testimony and therefore
not includable in the 40/10 time test. The inclusion of (3) as administrative testimony depends upon the purpose of the negotiations. I
cannot respond without more information regarding the type of negotiations.

While some of the above items are clearly excludable from the 40/10
time test of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(e)(A)1., any time spent by an
attorney on items (4) and (5) above would be includable in the time
tests of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(2)(A)2. and 3.

Please contact me if I can be of further assistance.

Sincerely,


J. Richard Eichman
Accounting Specialist
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

791145

LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

STANDARD OIL BUILDING
225 BUSH STREET

LONDON OFFICE
105 VICTORIA STREET, NINTH FLOOR
LONDON SWlE 6QU

TELEX 34743
CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
TELECOPIER TEL (415) 398-2906

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA

TELEPHONE: 01-628-4821
CABLE ADDRESS: EVANSLAW LONDON SWI
TELEX: 916556

MAIL ADDRESS: P.O. BOX 7880, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94120

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

983-1585

April 20, 1979

Political Reform Act of 1974 -
Lobbyist Registration
Requirements


Mr. J. Richard Eichman
Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Dear Rich:

     As I mentioned to you during our recent telephone
conversation, I would very much appreciate advice concerning
the proper computation of hours for the purposes of the time
test set out in 2 Cal.Admin. Code 18239(e)(2)(A)(1). This
test provides that any person who spends a total of 40 hours,
including 10 hours in direct communication, influencing
or attempting to influence legislative or administrative
action in a period consisting of two consecutive calendar
months shall be deemed to satisfy the "substantial or regular"
portion of the definition of "lobbyist" set out in Government
Code section 82039.

     This firm employs attorneys, some of whom from
time to time render services to clients in connection with
direct communication with a legislative agency or elected
state official for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action. Accordingly, this firm files periodic
reports pursuant to 2 Cal.Admin. Code 18620 as a person
spending $250 or more to influence legislative or administrative action. Although up to this time no attorney employed
by this firm has engaged in sufficient direct communication
with legislative or administrative officials such that any
attorney would have to register as a lobbyist under the Act,
it is possible that an attorney might have to do so in the
future. Accordingly, advice is requested pertaining to the
satisfaction of the 40/10 hours test heretofore referred to.

While it is clear what constitutes direct communication for the purpose of satisfying the 10 hour portion of the test, it is less clear what other time is includible for the purposes of the 40 hour threshold. Specifically, I note that 2 Cal.Admin. Code 18239(e)(3)(A), in defining the terms "influencing or attempting to influence legislative action" and "communicating directly" for purposes of the time and compensation tests, specifically states that such time does not include (1) administrative testimony (subject to the $10 gift rule) which is defined as acting as counsel in and preparing written submissions which become part of the record of any regulatory or administrative agency's public proceeding, (2) travel time, and (3) "time spent on research, i.e., the gathering of information, statistics, studies and analyses, the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs, memoranda or the preparation of bill analyses."

For the purposes of illustrating our question, please assume the following facts:

In a period of two consecutive months, an attorney spends 30 hours advising his client in connection with the adoption of a proposed regulation by a state agency. In the process of rendering this assistance, the attorney engages in legal research, analyzes various studies pertaining to the regulation, and sends letters and memoranda to the client to help educate and prepare the client for off-the-record negotiations with the agency. In addition, the attorney suggests language to be included in administrative testimony and he outlines various strategies for its presentation. The attorney also meets with the client to discuss the foregoing matters. Finally, the attorney spends 10 hours or more in direct communication with an agency official in connection with this matter.

While it seems clear in the above example that the attorney will have met the 10 hour portion of the 40/10 hour test, we conclude that the 30 hours spent on analysis and research-oriented activities may not count toward the 40 hour threshold. In other words, it would appear that the attorney in the above example would not have to register as a lobbyist.

2.

I appreciate your assistance on this matter. I will look forward to seeing you again on my next trip to Sacramento. Needless to say, we found your seminar on lobbying and disclosure most enlightening.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Frederick K. Lowell
for
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro

3.

Admin. Code 18239(e)(A)1. The specific activities included: (1) Engaging in legal research. (2) Analyzing various studies pertaining to the regulation. (3) Educating and preparing the client for off the record negotiations with the agency. (4) Suggesting language to be included in the administrative testimony. (5) Outlining various strategies for presentation of administrative testimony. (6) Meeting with the client to discuss the above matters.
sections.conclusion: Items (1) and (2) would be considered time spent on research and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. Items (4) and (5) would also be considered administrative testimony and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. The inclusion of (3) as administrative testimony depends upon the purpose of the negotiations and requires more information. Any time spent by an attorney on items (4) and (5) would be includable in the time tests of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(2)(A)2. and 3.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether specific activities by staff attorneys—including legal research, client analysis, preparation for negotiations, testimony language suggestions, and strategy discussions—count toward the 40/10 hour lobbyist time threshold under California regulations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC concluded that legal research and analysis (items 1-2) are excluded from the 40/10 hour test, as are administrative testimony activities (items 4-5). Item (3) regarding client preparation requires additional information, but items (4) and (5) would count under alternative time thresholds.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with some formatting issues and garbled text at bottom pages. Core question and conclusion sections are clearly discernible. Letter includes substantial incoming request text embedded in response, showing the full question asked by Mr. Lowell.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether specific activities of staff attorneys rendering services to clients would be included in determining if the attorneys qualified as lobbyists, with particular concern focused upon the time test set out in 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(e)(A)1. The specific activities included: (1) Engaging in legal research. (2) Analyzing various studies pertaining to the regulation. (3) Educating and preparing the client for off the record negotiations with the agency. (4) Suggesting language to be included in the administrative testimony. (5) Outlining various strategies for presentation of administrative testimony. (6) Meeting with the client to discuss the above matters.

CONCLUSION: Items (1) and (2) would be considered time spent on research and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. Items (4) and (5) would also be considered administrative testimony and therefore not includable in the 40/10 time test. The inclusion of (3) as administrative testimony depends upon the purpose of the negotiations and requires more information. Any time spent by an attorney on items (4) and (5) would be includable in the time tests of 2 Cal. Admin. Code 18239(2)(A)2. and 3.

embedding.summary: FPPC Accounting Specialist advises attorney at Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro on whether various staff attorney activities qualify as lobbyist work under the 40/10 hour time test for determining lobbyist status. The letter clarifies that research, analysis, and certain testimony preparation activities are excluded from the primary time calculation.
